data.title: Nikkei May Fall Amid Uncertainty Over Mideast Conflict — Market Talk
data.description:
1951 ET - Japanese stocks may fall as uncertainty over the Middle East conflict persists. Nikkei futures open at 59125 on the SGX, down 140 points from Wednesday and 840 points lower from Tuesday. Japanese markets were closed on Wednesday for a public holiday. The dollar is at 160.16 yen, compared with Y159.20 as of Tuesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on Japanese corporate earnings as well as developments in the Middle East. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 1.0% to 59917.46 on Tuesday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

2241 ET - Australian stocks are on course to extend their longest losing streak since 2022, with local futures pointing 0.8% lower ahead of Thursday's session. U.S. equities provided a soft lead as the DJIA and S&P 500 lost ground amid higher energy prices and rising bond yields. Australia's S&P/ASX 200 is coming off a 0.3% loss that took its losing streak to seven days. It hasn't finished lower for eight consecutive sessions since 2018. Ahead of the open, miner South32 said the development of the Taylor deposit in southern Arizona will take longer and cost more to build than previously thought. Supermarket operator Woolworths said third-quarter sales were up 4.5% from a year earlier. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1725 ET - Traders weren't really interested in what Bank of Canada Gov. Tiff Macklem was saying about the decision to hold rates steady, says Derek Holt, economist at Bank of Nova Scotia. The BOC's communications, he argues, were "stale on arrival," with traders instead eyeing higher interest rates due to sharply higher oil prices on fresh trouble in the U.S.-Iran conflict. Expectations for at least two rate hikes in Canada rose in overnight-index swap trading, with Brent and WTI trading above $100 a barrel. Holt says Macklem opening the door to future rate changes — both hikes and cuts — suggests the probability of policy change is higher. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1658 ET - The Fed committee is moving more hawkish over time, EY-Parthenon Chief Economist Gregory Daco tells WSJ. He says he wouldn't be surprised if the next FOMC statement included dual-sided language if tensions in the Middle East persist. Daco adds that markets are broadly aligned with the Fed's reaction function, pointing to little appetite for easing rates this year.(jessica.coacci@wsj.com)

1612 ET - When Chair Jerome Powell said in his press conference, "Nobody's calling for a rate hike right now," Diane Swonk chief economist at KPMG said that applies only to this policy meeting. "This is more than an oil shock," she said. "It has echoes of the pandemic in that it's roiling supply chains the world over." After the Fed's FOMC statement drew dissents from four of 12 members, Swonk said incoming chair Kevin Warsh is walking into a splintered Fed, "which we knew already." (jessica.coacci@wsj.com)

1554 ET - President Trump picked Kevin Warsh to lead the Fed in part because Warsh persuaded the president he was aligned with Trump's demands for lower interest rates. Not all GOP lawmakers appear aligned on that point. One Republican senator on the banking committee--which oversees the Fed--told reporters Wednesday that the central bank may need to raise rates to snuff out inflation. "Inflation is very pernicious, and it's kind of like a tick," Sen. John Kennedy of Louisiana told reporters before voting to advance Warsh's nomination. "If you get a tick, the best way to handle it is to get that SOB off of you immediately." (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1549 ET - Treasury yields reach their highest levels in a month as the Fed holds and keeps dovish language in the statement, while three policymakers vote for a more hawkish tone. Kevin Warsh's nomination advances in the Senate and he is likely to chair the next meeting, when another hold is expected. Advanced 1Q GDP growth estimate is expected at an annualized 2.2% pace, in a WSJ consensus. March 12-month PCE inflation is expected to accelerate to 3.5% from 2.8%. Weekly jobless claims are forecast to tick lower to 212,000 from 214,000. The 10-year adds 0.063 percentage point to 4.415% and the two-year rises 0.089 p.p. to 3.932%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1529 ET - When asked whether incoming Fed Chair Kevin Warsh will stand up to political pressure from President Trump, Jerome Powell says, "I'll take him at his word." Trump has been calling for more rate cuts. Warsh faced questions about whether he would align with Trump's push for lower rates at his confirmation hearing. (jessica.coacci@wsj.com)

1512 ET - When asked whether a Supreme Court ruling on the Trump administration's attempt to remove Governor Lisa Cook would factor into his decision on when to leave the Fed's board, Fed Chair Jerome Powell says, "I wasn't thinking of it as such, but no, not really." He adds that he was a governor for six years, and he knows what that is like. He says this is and will be a very normal, standard kind of a transition process. (jessica.coacci@wsj.com)

1500 ET - Departing Fed Chair Powell's comments keep Treasury yields rising as he highlights the uncertainties ahead. Powell says he will stay on the board for now, as Kevin Warsh replaces him as the chair. The bond selloff is also fueled by the post-meeting statement, which "showed that the theme of the two-day meeting was clearly about the risk that the war in Iran has brought to both sides of the central bank's mandate," B. Riley Wealth's Art Hogan writes. "One gets the sense that there will be several meetings at least before we see any rate changes by the FOMC." The 10-year yield is at 4.422%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1426 ET - Treasury yields tick higher as the Fed keeps interest rates unchanged, as expected. A surprise that likely fueled the bond selloff is that three FOMC members voted against language suggesting the Fed's next move is more likely to be a cut than a hike. The statement's dovish tone is maintained, but the dissent indicates incoming Chair Warsh may have a hard time convincing his colleagues to cut rates. Governor Stephen Miran again votes for a rate cut. The 10-year yield reaches 4.414% from 4.394% before the rate decision. The two-year rises to 3.941% from 3.906%. The dollar strengthens and the WSJ Dollar Index rises 0.4%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1415 ET - Major cryptocurrencies that started the day higher are now firmly down following the Federal Reserve's decision to keep interest rates unchanged at 3.5% to 3.75%. Shaking investor confidence is that the board appears more divided in this decision than it has in any since 1992 — with four members of the board dissenting, three of which backed the rate decision but objected to retaining language regarding the "easing bias" that has suggested a more likely future rate cut than rate hike. With the high level of dissension, traders of cryptocurrency assets are opting to lock in recent profits instead. Bitcoin is down 1.8% to $75,400, ethereum falls 2.4%, and XRP is down 2.2%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: DBS First-Quarter Net Profit Rose on Strong Wealth Management Performance
data.description:
(22:43 GMT) *DBS Group 1Q Net S$2.93B Vs. Net S$2.90B >D05.SG
(22:43 GMT) *DBS Group 1Q Total Income S$5.95B Vs. S$5.91B >D05.SG
(22:44 GMT) *DBS Group 1Q Net Interest Income S$3.48B Vs. S$3.72B >D05.SG
(22:45 GMT) *DBS Group 1Q Net Fee & Commission Income S$1.48B Vs. S$1.28B >D05.SG
(22:47 GMT) *DBS Group: Outlook Uncertain Due to Iran War, Potential Second-Order Effects >D05.SG
(22:49 GMT) *DBS Group: Bank's Stress Test Indicate Portfolio Remains Sound >D05.SG
(23:51 GMT) DBS First-Quarter Net Profit Rose on Strong Wealth Management Performance

By Amanda Lee

DBS Group's first-quarter net profit rose mainly due to its robust wealth management performance.

Net profit for the three months ended March increased 1.1% from the year-earlier period to 2.93 billion Singapore dollars, equivalent to US$2.29 billion, Singapore's largest bank said Thursday.

That beat the S$2.78 billion consensus estimate of analysts polled by Visible Alpha.

Total income during the quarter was up 0.7% at S$5.95 billion from a year ago. Net interest income — the difference between what banks earn on loans and pay on deposits — for its commercial book was 6.6% lower at S$3.48 billion.

The bank, which is also Southeast Asia's largest by assets, said commercial book net fee and commission income rose 16% to S$1.48 billion.

Its commercial book treasury customer sales and other income rose 9.85% to S$602 million.

The bank's strong wealth management performance drove its fee income and treasury customer sales to new highs, it said.

DBS declared an ordinary dividend of S$0.66 per share and a capital return dividend of S$0.15 per share for the first quarter.

"While the Iran war and its potential second-order effects have added uncertainty to the outlook, our stress tests indicate that our credit portfolio remains sound," said Chief Executive Tan Su Shan.

DBS is the first of Singapore's three major lenders to report earnings. United Overseas Bank and Oversea-Chinese Banking Corp. are scheduled to release their results in May.

Write to Amanda Lee at amanda.lee@wsj.com

data.title: Sandisk shares have soared over 3,000% in a year. Is a stock split next?
data.description:
By Christine Ji

The S&P 500's top-performing stock of the year has crossed the $1,000 threshold, fueling some speculation that management could announce a 10-for-1 stock split

Sandisk has become a popular AI name among retail investors since rejoining the S&P 500 in 2025.

As Sandisk prepares to report earnings on Thursday, Wall Street is expecting another blowout quarter powered by strong artificial-intelligence demand. Some on social media are wondering if the report will also bring the announcement of a stock split that brings the high-flying share price back down to earth for retail investors.

A shortage of memory and storage solutions has led shares of Sandisk (SNDK) to rally 3,130% in the past year and trade at $1,064. Up over 350% year to date, Sandisk is the best-performing member of the S&P 500 Index SPX in 2026.

The four-figure price tag makes Sandisk a prime candidate for a stock split, in which a company divides its shares into smaller units to lower the price and increase liquidity. The company's overall market capitalization remains unaffected.

For Sandisk, a 10-for-1 stock split would reset the price to the low triple digits, which can be psychologically more appealing to retail investors. A lower price would also improve liquidity in the options market, as each standard option contract represents 100 underlying shares.

Sandisk has become an increasingly popular AI stock among retail investors ever since it spun out of Western Digital (WDC) in February 2025. Net retail buying in the stock has surged alongside the stock price, with hundreds of millions of dollars of shares exchanging hands daily, according to an April report from JPMorgan analyst Arun Jain. Sandisk consistently ranks among the top stocks and options traded by retail investors, Jain added.

Sandisk did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Ahead of the report, some social-media users have speculated about the potential for a split.

There's past precedent for stock splits among AI infrastructure names. In June 2024, Nvidia announced a 10-for-1 stock split, kicking off a trend among semiconductor companies. In the following months, Broadcom (AVGO), Lam Research (LRCX) and Super Micro Computer (SMCI) followed suit with their own 10-for-1 splits. However, the momentum leading up to the stock split hasn't always been sustainable: Shares of Super Micro initially popped on the news but are down 37% since the split.

Read: Micron and Sandisk shares could soar further thanks to a software-like subscription model

While a stock split could certainly be in the cards, management might hesitate while the entire memory industry is in the middle of a massive transformation, Ed Yardeni, president of financial-research firm Yardeni Research, told MarketWatch.

Semiconductor stocks have historically been cyclical, experiencing boom-and-bust phases from supply-demand mismatches. Winning stocks can quickly plummet once "irrational exuberance is taken out of the stock," Yardeni said.

The narrative surrounding memory chips could evolve once major AI labs such as OpenAI and Anthropic go public later this year. If their financial disclosures paint a different demand picture than the one currently expected by investors, then prices could correct, according to Yardeni. The fragility of the AI trade was on full display Tuesday, when a Wall Street Journal report saying that OpenAI missed internal revenue targets sent shares of Oracle (ORCL), CoreWeave (CRWV), Microsoft (MSFT), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Broadcom lower.

Read: Oracle, Nvidia and other buzzy tech stocks fall as the 'OpenAI complex' comes under pressure

Demand for Sandisk shares remains elevated at the current price, meaning that there's no immediate necessity to lower the price to attract more buyers, Yardeni added. But he's not ruling out the possibility completely. As a result of the AI boom, "it's arguable that memory chips in particular are no longer cyclical and becoming a long-term growth industry instead," Yardeni said.

Melius Research analyst Ben Reitzes believes AI could permanently elevate demand for memory, leading customers to "subscribe" to multiyear commitments like a software business model. As a result, Sandisk's valuation multiples could double or triple from current levels, Reitzes wrote in a note earlier this week.

-Christine Ji

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: SoftBank Plots IPO for New Robotics Venture — WSJ
data.description:
By Kate Clark

SoftBank Group is forming a new venture focused on the intersection of artificial intelligence and robotics, an area CEO Masayoshi Son sees as the "next frontier" for the Japanese conglomerate.

The new venture, called Roze AI, aims to make the physical buildout of AI infrastructure more efficient, including by using autonomous robotics to build data centers, people familiar with the matter said. It plans to take the new venture public as soon as the second half of the year, the people said.

SoftBank plans to bundle some of the Japanese conglomerate's existing energy, land and infrastructure bets together under Roze, one of the people said. It could tap SoftBank's energy unit to power those projects, though SB Energy will remain a separate business, the people familiar with the plans said.

SoftBank is moving quickly to prepare Roze for an IPO at a time when investors are enthusiastic about the AI boom and eagerly awaiting potential public listings from hot startups such as SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic. Son's company is one of many investors piling into physical AI, the use of AI in real-world systems like robots and manufacturing.

The venture represents the latest in a long history of risky, capital-intensive bets by SoftBank. Son has touted the potential of robotics in the past, though that enthusiasm has at times led to missteps. One investment, Zume Pizza, shut down and SoftBank halted production of a humanoid robot named Pepper in 2021.

More recently, SoftBank has shifted toward industrial robotics. In October, SoftBank agreed to acquire ABB's robotics division, which develops robotics arms used in manufacturing, in a $5.4 billion deal.

SoftBank is planning to hold an analyst day at one of its data center facilities in Texas in July to drum up interest for a Roze public offering and has hired KPMG to prepare the financials and documents required ahead of an IPO, the people familiar with the plans said.

Bilal Safeer, an executive at SoftBank-owned Arm, is currently serving as interim CFO of the venture.

To fund recent investments, SoftBank has also discussed selling a portion of its stake in Intel, people familiar with the discussions said. Last year, SoftBank sold its stake in Nvidia for $5.8 billion to free up more cash to invest in OpenAI.

SoftBank is one of OpenAI's largest investors and earlier this year committed another $30 billion to the company as part of its $122 billion funding round. The company has also been making a push into data centers, including through the purchase of data-center investment firm DigitalBridge and participation in AI infrastructure project Stargate alongside OpenAI and Oracle, though that project has faced setbacks.

Write to Kate Clark at kate.clark@wsj.com
